title: Hanging an internal door
subtitle: Plane to fit, mark and cut the hinge housings, hang and check
excerpt: How to hang a new internal door in an existing frame: planing the door to fit the opening, marking out and cutting hinge housings in door and frame, fitting the hinges, and hanging the door to close square and latch cleanly.
sourceNotes: Standard UK joinery practice from Newey and Drage Practical Carpentry and Joinery and the City & Guilds Site Carpentry Level 2 syllabus. Hinge positioning and clearance dimensions from BS 8214.

content.text: Hanging an internal door is the benchmark carpentry job on a building site and the single most-asked-for domestic woodwork skill. The sequence is measuring the opening, fitting the door to the frame by planing, marking and cutting the hinge housings, and screwing the hinges home. A door hung badly binds, drags, or fails to latch; a door hung well closes with a single finger.
Measuring the opening
Step 1, measure the frame opening.

Measure the clear opening height and width at three points each: top, middle, and bottom for width; left, centre, and right for height. Use the smallest dimension. Standard internal openings are 2040 × 626 mm for a 610 mm (2 foot) door or 2040 × 826 mm for a 813 mm (2 foot 8 inch) door. The door should be 3 mm smaller in both dimensions for clearance.
Fitting the door to the frame
Step 2, mark the door to size.

Stand the door in the opening and mark the amount to remove at the top, bottom, and sides with a pencil. Allow 3 mm clearance at the top and sides, and 8 mm at the bottom if there is no carpet; 12 mm if carpet will be fitted.
Step 3, plane to size.

Plane the hinge side perfectly straight and square first; the latch side is adjusted to fit once the hinges are hung. Plane across the grain at the top and bottom edges with a sharp plane, working from the corners toward the centre to avoid splitting the stile. Take light passes and check frequently in the frame. The door should drop into the frame and sit with even gaps all round.
Hinge positions
Step 4, mark the hinge positions.

Standard UK internal door hinge positions for two 75 mm butt hinges: top hinge 150 mm from the top of the door, bottom hinge 225 mm from the bottom. A three-hinge door adds a centre hinge halfway between. Mark a horizontal line at each hinge position on both the door stile and the corresponding position on the frame jamb.
Cutting the hinge housings
Step 5, mark out the housing.
Hold the hinge leaf against the door stile at the marked position and scribe around it with a marking knife or sharp pencil. The housing depth is the full thickness of one hinge leaf.
Step 6, cut the housing.

Chisel across the grain at the housing ends to the marked depth, then pare the waste out with the chisel flat-side down. Work in thin layers. The housing must be flat and even so the hinge leaf sits flush with the surface. Check with the hinge before drilling the screw holes.
Step 7, repeat on the frame jamb.
Cut matching housings on the door frame at the same hinge positions. Fit the hinges to the door first by drilling pilot holes and screwing in all screws.
Hanging the door
Step 8, hang the door on the frame.

Prop the door in the opening at the correct height using wedges at the bottom to hold the clearance. Line up the hinge leaves with their housings in the frame and drive one screw per hinge to hold the door. Swing the door and check the gaps around the frame. Adjust by deepening or packing one or more housings if needed. When the door closes and latches cleanly, drive the remaining screws.
Common mistakes
Door binding on the latch side near the top.

The top hinge housing is too deep, causing the door to hinge knuckle to act as a fulcrum and push the latch side into the frame stop. Pack the top hinge housing with a sliver of card to raise it slightly.
Door not closing against the stop at the top.
The bottom hinge is too deep or the top is too shallow. Deepen the top hinge housing by a fraction or pack the bottom hinge.
Screws pulling out of the hinge housing.
Drive the screws into end grain of softwood or into a damaged frame and the housing can fail. Plug the holes with short lengths of dowel glued in, allow the glue to cure, and redrill.
